coredata.dc:title: The distinguished long-term friction reduction and anti-wear functions of amphipathic carbon dots originated from lauryl gallate
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Weiwei
Wang, Baogang
Wen, Guiqiang
Li, Yufeng
coredata.dc:description: The tribolgical performance of amphipathic carbon dots (A-CDs) originated from lauryl gallate as lubricant additives of polyethylene glycol (PEG200) was investigated in detail under the ball-on-disk reciprocating slide mode and steel/steel contact. The A-CDs exhibited distinguished long-term friction reduction and wear resistance functions under specific conditions. Typically, 2 wt% of A-CDs made the average friction coefficient and wear volume of wear track lubricated with PEG200 reduce by 51.4% and 84.4%, respectively, under the mild load of 40 N and long duration of 200 min. The worn surface analyses demonstrated that the A-CDs could be uninterruptedly deposited and smeared onto the contact areas of friction pairs, which induced the gradually formation of compact boundary lubrication films. Under the mild load, the homogeneous lubrication films were thick and robust enough to absolutely avoid the direct collisions of asperities on the sliding interfaces, well accounting for the impressive long-term friction-reducing and anti-wear abilities of A-CDs. The A-CDs offer great application potential in the field of lubricant additives due to their high lubricating performance, long service life and environmentally friendliness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Lauryl gallate
Friction
Wear
Lubricant additive
Polyethylene glycol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tribological tests and worn surface analyses
Dispersion stability of A-CDs in PEG200
Tribological performance of A-CDs as additives of PEG200
Worn surface analyses and lubricating mechanism of A-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51975493 ) and the Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project of China ( 2015M582567 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) high-resolution TEM images of A-CDs. Inset of (b): particle size distribution of A-CDs and high-resolution TEM image of single particle.
FTIR spectra of (a) lauryl gallate and (b) A-CDs.
Schematic diagram of synthesis process of A-CDs.

(a) Optical photos of PEG200 and A-CDs/PEG200 suspension (1.0 wt%) after standing for 30 days at 25, −15 and 150 °C and centrifuging for 30 min at 10000 rpm under room light and UV light (365 nm). (b) UV–vis absorbance spectra of the A-CDs/PEG200 suspension (1.0 wt%) after standing for 1–8 days at room temperature.

(a) Friction coefficient curves of the A-CDs/PEG200 suspensions with different concentrations (c) and lauryl gallate solution (2.0 wt%) in PEG200 (load: 40 N). (b) Mean friction coefficient and wear volume lubricated with A-CDs/PEG200 suspension varying with the increasing c (load: 40 N).
Friction coefficient curves of A-CDs (2.0 wt%)/PEG200 suspension after standing for 1 day, 3 and 8 days (load: 40 N).
(a) Mean friction coefficient and (b) wear volume lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not (blank) varying with the increased load from 20 to 100 N.

(a) Friction coefficient curves of PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not (blank) under a prolonged duration of 200 min (b) Mean wear volumes of PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not (blank) under the duration of 20 and 200 min, respectively.

Microscopic images and corresponding two- and three-dimensional profiles of the wear tracks lubricated with (a) blank PEG200, (b) lauryl gallate solution (2.0 wt%) in PEG200 and (c) A-CDs (2.0 wt%)/PEG200 suspension (load: 40 N; duration: 20 min).
SEM images of the wear tracks lubricated with (a) PEG200 and (b) PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs (load: 40 N; duration: 20 min).
The Fe2p, C1s and O1s XPS spectra of the worn surfaces lubricated with (a) PEG200 and (b) PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs (load: 40 N; duration: 20 min).
Schematic plot of lubricating mechanism of A-CDs in PEG200.
Elemental compositions (derived from the EDS analyses) of worn surfaces lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not (load: 40 N; duration: 20 min).
Elemental compositions (derived from the XPS analyses) of worn surfaces lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not (load: 40 N; duration: 20 min).

head.ce:title: The distinguished long-term friction reduction and anti-wear functions of amphipathic carbon dots originated from lauryl gallate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Panzhihua University, Panzhihua 617000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Panzhihua University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Panzhihua University, Panzhihua 617000, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Triumph Graphite Carbon Co., Ltd, Panzhihua 617000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Triumph Graphite Carbon Co., Ltd, Panzhihua 617000, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The tribolgical performance of amphipathic carbon dots (A-CDs) originated from lauryl gallate as lubricant additives of polyethylene glycol (PEG200) was investigated in detail under the ball-on-disk reciprocating slide mode and steel/steel contact. The A-CDs exhibited distinguished long-term friction reduction and wear resistance functions under specific conditions. Typically, 2 wt% of A-CDs made the average friction coefficient and wear volume of wear track lubricated with PEG200 reduce by 51.4% and 84.4%, respectively, under the mild load of 40 N and long duration of 200 min. The worn surface analyses demonstrated that the A-CDs could be uninterruptedly deposited and smeared onto the contact areas of friction pairs, which induced the gradually formation of compact boundary lubrication films. Under the mild load, the homogeneous lubrication films were thick and robust enough to absolutely avoid the direct collisions of asperities on the sliding interfaces, well accounting for the impressive long-term friction-reducing and anti-wear abilities of A-CDs. The A-CDs offer great application potential in the field of lubricant additives due to their high lubricating performance, long service life and environmentally friendliness.

ce:section.ce:para: Liquid lubricants have provided indispensable supports for the smooth operation of mechanical devices and the rapid development of modern industries [1]. The low amounts of lubricant additives, including friction modifiers, anti-wear agents, antioxidants, rust inhibitors, detergents and thickening agents, are the quintessence of high-grade lube oils. With the dramatically increasing consumption of lube oils, the pollution issues triggered by the leakage and emission of lubricants are attracting a lot of attentions. Thus, the development of high-performance green lubricants including environmentally friendly base oils and additives is urgently needed.

Polyethylene glycol (PEG) is a synthetic base oil, which has found wide applications in gear oils, metal working fluids, brake fluids and compressor lubricants since it is eco-friendly and degradable [1–4]. The lubricating ability of blank PEG is fairly poor under the harsh boundary lubrication regime and hence the friction reduction and anti-wear additives are absolutely essential for PEG. Owing to the partial polarity of PEG, the ionic liquids have been extensively applied as the additives of PEG because of their good comparability with base oil, negligible volatility and non-flammability [5–20]. The ionic liquids can form denser double-electric layer structures on the metal rubbing surfaces by physically electrostatic adsorption. If the friction conditions are harsh, the above physical absorption films react with the metal surfaces to form the tribochemcial films, which greatly reduce the friction and wear of friction pairs. Thanks to the versatile designability of ionic liquids, the drawbacks of corrosivity and easy oxidation of ionic liquids were commendably solved by Cai et al. through introducing target groups into ionic liquid molecules [7–9]. The pollution risks of ionic liquids were largely alleviated by Gusain et al. via synthesizing the halogen, phosphorus and sulfur-free ionic liquids [13–15]. The bio-compatible ionic liquids were also synthesized by Khatri et al. by means of amino acids [21]. Although many progresses have been made on improving the defects of ionic liquids, the high cost and unsatisfactory friction-reducing and wear resistance performance of ionic liquids under the harsh lubricating conditions still significantly hinder their practical applications as additives of PEG.

The nanomaterials have been the another promising additives of PEG due to their distinct rolling, mending and polishing effects and strong film-building ability on the rubbing surfaces [22–27]. The boundary lubrication films formed by deposition and/or sintering of nanomaterials were usually thick and robust enough to tremendously reduce friction and wear of friction pairs under harsh conditions. Among the various nanomaterials, such as metals [2,28,29], oxides [30–32], and carbon materials [33–50], the carbon dots (CDs) are attracting the most attentions owing to their uniform and small particle size, tunable composition and structure, designable amphiphilicity and unique self-lubricating effect [51–54]. To realize the good comparability between CDs and PEG, the expensive ionic liquids were commonly used as the modifiers or doping agents. In some cases, some pollution elements such as Mo, P, S and F were introduced into the CDs [42–45]. The eco-friendly CDs completely composed of C and O elements were seldom synthesized as additives of PEG200 [38]. For instance, the organophilic CDs has been synthesized by pyrolysis of lauryl gallate as non-linear optical materials [55]. Therefore, syntheses of CDs only containing C and O and then exploiting their tribological behaviors as additives of PEG are exceedingly meaningful.

Inspired by the possibly favorable comparability between the former lauryl gallate-generated CDs and PEG, the amphipathic CDs (A-CDs) only containing C and O were synthesized by one-step pyrolysis of lauryl gallate. The tribological performance of A-CDs as additives of PEG200 was investigated in detail under the ball-on-disk reciprocating slide mode and steel/steel contact. The effects of additive concentration (c), load and friction duration on the tribological behaviors of A-CDs were clarified. The morphologies and compositions of worn surfaces of wear tracks lubricated with PEG200 containing A-CDs or not were analyzed. The reasonable lubricating mechanism of A-CDs as additives was discussed according to the experimental findings and results of worn surface analyses.

Lauryl gallate (>98%) were provided by the Aladdin Shanghai Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). Anhydrous alcohol (AR), chloroform (AR), and polyethylene glycol (AR) with an average molecular weight of 200 g/mol (PEG200) was purchased from the Chengdu Kelong Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). All the chemicals were used as received.

The A-CDs were synthesized according to the reported method with a few modifications [55]. Briefly, a ceramic crucible containing 3 g of lauryl gallate was put into a heating oven. Then the crucible was calcined for 3 h at 270 °C under air atmosphere. After cooling down to the room temperature, 40 mL of anhydrous alcohol was used to dissolve the product in crucible. The gained black suspension was filtered for 3 times to eliminate the sediments. After removing the alcohol by rotary evaporation, the crude product was obtained. To further purify the crude product, 40 mL of chloroform was used to re-disperse the product. The insoluble impurities were removed by the filtration. The chloroform was eliminated by rotary evaporation and freeze-drying treatments. Finally, the A-CDs were gained as the brownish black powder.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of A-CDs were recorded on a FEI Tecnai G20 system operating at 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of lauryl gallate and A-CDs were obtained on a FTIR spectrophotometer (WQF-520). The thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) of A-CDs were gained on a thermogravimetric analyzer (STA449F3) under Ar atmosphere. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of A-CDs/PEG200 suspension (1.0 wt%) standing for different times were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (U-4100).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological tests and worn surface analyses
ce:section.ce:para: Tribological properties of A-CDs as the additives of PEG200 were evaluated on a Bruker universal mechanical tester (UMT) platform under the ball-on-disk reciprocating sliding mode and steel/steel contact. The upper running ball (12.7 mm in diameter) and the lower stationary disk (50 mm × 35 mm×4 mm in size) were both made of AISI 52100 bearing steel with the hardness of about 59–61 HRC. The applied load was 20–100 N, i.e., the initial Hertzian contact pressure was about 1.38–2.36 GPa. The average sliding speed was about 10 mm/s. The duration was 20 or 120 min. The amplitude was 5 mm. All the tests were conducted at the room temperature and under atmospheric environment. The balls and disks were ultrasonically cleaned in acetone for 10 min prior to the frictional test. Then 0.2 mL of PEG200 containing A-CDs or not was dropped onto the ball-disk contact region. The friction coefficients were recorded continuously by a linked computer. Each frictional test was repeated for at least three times. The mean friction coefficient was calculated as the average value of all data points on the friction coefficient curve. After the frictional test, the lower disk was cleaned in acetone by ultrasonic treatment. The two- and three-dimensional profiles of wear tracks of the lower disks were gained by Bruker Contour GT-K 3D optical microscope equipped with a white-light interferometer. The wear volume of lower disk was calculated as the product of the integral area of two-dimensional profile and the amplitude. The morphology and elemental composition of the worn surface was probed by a JSM-7500 F scanning electron microscope (SEM) with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250) with a mono-chromatized Al Ka X-ray source (1486.71 eV).

The TEM image shown in Fig. 1a confirms that the A-CDs with an almost spherical morphology are successfully synthesized by one-step pyrolysis of lauryl gallate. Many lattice fringes can be clearly observed in the high-resolution TEM image of the single particle (inset of Fig. 1b), reflecting the fairly high degree of graphitization of A-CDs. The high content of graphite phase structures in A-CDs can be attributed to the relatively high pyrolysis temperature [56]. The particle size of A-CDs is uniform with a narrow distribution range of 1.5–3.5 nm (inset of Fig. 1b). The average particle size of A-CDs is calculated as 2.6 nm in diameter by counting more than 100 particles.

To reveal the surface information of synthesized A-CDs, the FTIR spectra of lauryl gallate and A-CDs were gained and shown in Fig. 2. In the Fig. 2a, the feature absorption bands of lauryl gallate can be well found. The strong bands at 3455 and 3349 cm−1 are attributed to the O-H stretching vibrations of phenolic hydroxyl groups. The bands at 2921 and 2850 cm−1 are belonging to the C-H stretching vibrations of methyl and methylene groups. The bands at 1665 and 1610 cm−1 are assigned to the CO and CC stretching vibrations of ester groups and benzene rings, respectively. The appearance of vast bands among 1500–500 cm−1 further verifies the existence of long alkyl chain and benzene ring in lauryl gallate molecule. Many typical bands of lauryl gallate especially the bands at 3455 and 3349 cm−1 are disappeared in the spectrum of A-CDs (Fig. 2b). Meanwhile, the intensities of all bands are greatly weakened, meaning that the lauryl gallate molecules are decomposed during the pyrolysis process. The long alkyl chains should reserve on the surfaces of A-CDs as the strong bands at 2921 and 2850 cm−1 are observed in Fig. 2b. The weak bands at 1704 and 1604 cm−1 are also owing to the CO and CC stretching vibrations, respectively. The bands at 1459 and 1342 cm−1 are derived from the C-H deformation vibrations of methylene linked to ester groups and long alkyl chain groups, respectively, suggesting that the long alkyl chains are attached onto A-CDs by ester groups. The bands at 1459 and 1342 cm−1 caused by O-H and C-O deformation vibrations imply that there are some hydroxyl and ether groups on A-CDs. These results demonstrate that the surfaces of A-CDs are abundant in long alkyl chain and oxygen-containing groups.

The thermal stability of A-CDs was evaluated by the TGA analysis. The TGA curve of A-CDs shown in Fig. 3 illustrates two stages of weight loss with the increasing temperature. The first stage begins at about 160 °C, which can be attributed to the thermal decomposition of oxygen-containing groups on A-CDs. The second stage at about 302 °C can be due to the thermal decomposition of long alkyl chain groups.

The above characterization results provide some clues for the formation process of A-CDs. It has been widely accepted that the A-CDs synthesized by the bottom-up methods were usually derived from the thermal decomposition of various molecular precursors [51,53,57,58]. As shown in Fig. 4, the precursor of lauryl gallate should stepwise undergo three stages of dehydration, polymerization and carbonization at the high temperature of 270 °C. Then, the A-CDs, with uniform particle size, quite high degree of graphitization and abundant surface groups including long alkyl chain and some oxygen-containing groups, are gradually formed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion stability of A-CDs in PEG200
ce:section.ce:para: The alcohol and chloroform were used to extract the A-CDs during the synthesis processes, hinting that the synthesized A-CDs are amphiphilic. The amphiphilicity of A-CDs offers great promises for their good dispersion stability in different solvents. As expected, the A-CDs can be simply dispersed into PEG200 by only ultrasonic treatment without help of any dispersant ( Fig. 5a). The A-CDs/PEG200 suspension (1.0 wt%) seems like a transparent brown solution without any precipitates after 30 days of storage at 25, −15 and 150 °C and 30 min of centrifugation at 10000 rpm. Moreover, the suspension even emits the blue-green fluorescence after long-term storage and centrifugation. These phenomena jointly reflect the excellent dispersion stability of A-CDs in base oil, which is of key importance for their potential application as additives. The UV–vis spectra of the A-CDs/PEG200 suspension shown in Fig. 5b after 1–8 days of storage are almost completely overlapped, further verifying the excellent dispersion stability of A-CDs in PEG200.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological performance of A-CDs as additives of PEG200
ce:section.ce:para: The tribological performance of A-CDs as additives of PEG200 was evaluated under the ball-on-disk reciprocating slide mode and steel/steel contact. Fig. 6a gives the friction coefficient curves of bare PEG200, A-CDs/PEG200 suspensions and lauryl gallate solution (2.0 wt%) in PEG200. The curve of PEG200 fluctuates at a relatively high level between 0.13 and 0.17 due to its moderate lubricating ability. In sharp contrast, the curves of A-CDs/PEG200 suspensions are more stable and wave at a much lower level, verifying the impressive friction-reducing ability of A-CDs. All curves of suspensions illustrate a declining trend at the beginning 150 s, suggesting that the A-CDs can be quickly absorbed onto the rubbing surfaces to form the low shear lubrication films. Commonly, the formation of uniform lubrication film needs the proper c of nano-additives [24–26]. Hence, the A-CDs/PEG200 suspension with an optimum c of 2.0 wt% exports the lowest friction coefficient curve. As a reference, the curve of lauryl gallate solution (2.0 wt%) in PEG200 was also presented in Fig. 6a. The curve for lauryl gallate solution demonstrates an obvious rising trend between the 300–600 s, reflecting that the lubrication films formed by absorbing lauryl gallate are gradually destroyed during the friction process. These results further validate the superiority of A-CDs as additives. The specific influences of additive c on the tribological performance of A-CDs can be well clarified by the results shown in Fig. 6b. Both the mean friction coefficient and wear volume of A-CDs/PEG200 suspension are quickly reduce first and then increase lightly with the increasing c. According to the overall consideration of friction-reducing and anti-wear functions, the A-CDs exhibit the best tribological performance at the c of 2.0 wt%, that is, the friction coefficient and wear volume of PEG200 can be reduced by 46.7% and 50.0%, respectively.

Fig. 7 gives the friction coefficient curves of A-CDs (2.0 wt%)/PEG200 suspension after standing for 1 day, 3 and 8 days. All of the curves are tangled with each other especially after 300 s, reflecting that the tribological performance of A-CDs (2.0 wt%)/PEG200 suspension is stable after standing for a relatively long time. The stable performance of A-CDs as additives of PEG200 can be attributed to the excellent dispersion stability of A-CDs in PEG200.

As another important factor, the load can exert great effects on the tribologcial behaviors of A-CDs. The mean friction coefficient and wear volume values lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not under various loads were gained and shown in Fig. 8. It is not difficult to find that the differences of mean friction coefficient (Fig. 8a) and wear volume (Fig. 8b) between the blank PEG200 and A-CDs/PEG200 suspension (2.0 wt%) both gradually shrink with the increasing load. At the load of 20 N, the mean friction coefficient and wear volume of suspension are 51.8% and 70.9% lower than that of blank PEG200, respectively, whereas the friction and wear reductions triggered by 2.0 wt% of A-CDs are only 35.8% and 11.2% when the load is increased to 100 N. The attenuation of lubricating functions of A-CDs with increasing load can be due to the gradually failure of boundary lubrication film formed by A-CDs under the heavy loads. Generally, the A-CDs as additives of PEG200 can play their lubricating functions sufficiently under the mild loads.

At the optimum c and the mild load of 40 N, the service life of A-CDs as additives of PEG200 were evaluated by prolonging the duration from 20 min to 200 min. As shown in the Fig. 9a, the friction coefficient curve of PEG200 is high and rises continuously during the whole duration, meaning that the PEG200 itself can hardly form the effective lubrication films on the steel/steel rubbing surfaces. The friction coefficient curve of suspension is much low and declines persistently with the increasing friction time, determining the impressive durability of the boundary lubrication films formed by A-CDs. Generally, the friction coefficient reduction for PEG200 triggered by 2.0 wt% of A-CDs moderately rises from 46.7% to 51.4% with increasing duration from 20 min to 200 min. It is also worth noting that the wear resistance ability of A-CDs is further enhanced when the duration is prolonged (Fig. 9b). The wear volume reduction induced by A-CDs dramatically increases from 50.0% to 84.4% when the duration is prolonged from 20 min to 200 min. The improved friction reduction and wear resistance performance of A-CDs with increasing friction time directly reflects the relatively long service life of A-CDs, which is of key importance for their practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Worn surface analyses and lubricating mechanism of A-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To shed light on the lubricating mechanism of A-CDs as additives, the worn surfaces lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not were analyzed by 3D optical microscope, SEM, EDS and XPS technologies. As shown in Fig. 10a, the wear track of lower disk lubricated with blank PEG200 has a large width of 0.46 mm and a big depth of 2.30 µm. In addition, the noticeable scratches can be found on the surface of wear track, reflecting the weak wear resistance ability of PEG200. When 2.0 wt% of lauryl gallate was introduced into PEG200, the width and depth of wear track slightly reduce to 0.43 mm and 2.09 µm (Fig. 10b), respectively, indicating that the lauryl gallate as additives plays a mild anti-wear function. Once the A-CDs (2.0 wt%) was added into PEG200, the width and depth of wear track dramatically drop to 0.39 mm and 1.73 µm (Fig. 10c), respectively. Meanwhile, the surface of wear track is smooth with negligible scuffing, directly confirming the impressive wear resistance ability of A-CDs. The three-dimensional profiles of wear tracks shown in Fig. 10 visually reveal the huge wear volume reduction caused by the A-CDs (2.0 wt%), further determining the distinct anti-wear function of A-CDs.

The micro-topographies of worn surfaces of wear tracks lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not are revealed by SEM images ( Fig. 11). The wear track lubricated with blank PEG200 is wide with a rough surface (Fig. 11a). The deep scratches (marked out by the red arrows) and corrosion pits (marked out by the red circles) can be clearly observed on the worn surface, implying the occurrence of serious adhesive and abrasive wears. In striking contrast, the width of wear track lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs is remarkably narrowed (Fig. 11b). Moreover, the worn surface of wear track is so smooth that the scuffing and pits can hardly be found, suggesting the formation of protective boundary lubrication films on the rubbing surfaces. The boundary films are homogeneous and thick enough to completely avoid the direct collisions of asperities on rubbing surfaces and hence result in remarkable friction and wear reductions. The selected area EDS analyses were also carried out on the worn surfaces of wear tracks lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not, and the elemental compositions of worn surfaces were listed in Table 1. The content of C element on worn surface lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs is obviously higher than that lubricated with bare PEG200, confirming that the A-CDs have to participate in the formation of boundary lubrication films.

The specific information of boundary lubrication films on worn surfaces lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not was disclosed by XPS analyses and shown in Table 2 and Fig. 12. The overwhelmingly high contents of C and O elements on the worn surface lubricated with PEG200 (Table 2) imply that the tribochemical reactions must happen between the lubricant and steel surface to form some compounds containing C and O. Although the compounds are not enough to constitute the continuous lubrication film on the worn surface, large amounts of C and O can be detected because the detection depth of XPS technology is only several nm. The content of C on the worn surface lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs is apparently higher than that lubricated with PEG200, which can be attributed to the deposition of A-CDs on the worn surface. According to the fitting peaks shown in Fig. 12, the Fe, C and O species on the worn surfaces of wear tracks lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs or not are very similar. Fe element is existent in the form of oxides. However, the peak intensities of cementite (Fe3C) and iron oxides (FeO and Fe2O3) on worn surface lubricated with PEG200 containing 2.0 wt% of A-CDs are higher and lower than that lubricated with PEG200, respectively, which is well consistent with the results shown in Table 2. These results can also be ascribed to the participation of A-CDs in the formation of boundary lubrication films.

Based on the above experimental findings and characterization results, the lubricating mechanism of A-CDs as additives of PEG200 was discussed ( Fig. 13). During the friction process, the high contact pressure and tremendous amount of frictional heat corporately result in the tribochemcial reactions between lubricant and steel surface. The C and O compounds formed by PEG200 itself can hardly to form the continuous lubrication films on the rubbing surfaces. Once the A-CDs are introduced into the PEG200, the A-CDs with small particle size can gradually deposit and smear onto the rubbing surfaces and play the rolling, mending and polishing effects, which can be verified by the running-in period in Fig. 6a. The super dispersion stability of A-CDs in PEG200 supports the interrupted supply of A-CDs into the asperities of sliding contact area. Then the compact tribofilms composed of A-CDs and iron oxides (Table 2 and Fig. 12) are generated little by little on the rubbing surfaces, producing a smooth worn surface (Fig. 11b). The formed lubrication films are homogenous, thick and robust enough under the mild load, which can adequately prevent the rubbing surfaces from direct contact for a long time and hence bring about the remarkable long-term friction and wear reductions (Fig. 9).

The A-CDs with average particle size of 2.6 nm, relatively high degree of graphitization and abundant surface groups were conveniently synthesized by directly pyrolyzing the precursor of lauryl gallate. The A-CDs exhibited super dispersion stability in PEG200 owing to their amphipathic surface groups. As the additives of PEG200, the A-CDs demonstrated the distinguished friction reduction and wear resistance performance under the ball-on-disk reciprocating slide mode and steel/steel contact. The additive c and load both imposed significant influences on the tribological performance of A-CDs. The optimum c and proper load were determined to be 2.0 wt% and mild load (such as 20 and 40 N), respectively. At the c of 2.0 wt% and mild load of 40 N, both the friction-reducing and anti-wear abilities of A-CDs were prominently strengthened when the duration was prolonged from 20 min to 200 min. The results of worn surface analyses illustrated that the compact boundary lubrication films composed of A-CDs and iron oxides were formed on the rubbing surfaces when the PEG200 containing A-CDs was used as the lubricant. Under the mild load, the formed lubrication films are thick and robust enough to prevent the rubbing surfaces from direct contact for a long time, directly accounting for the distinguished long-term friction reduction and wear resistance functions of A-CDs.

Weiwei Tang: acquisition of data, analysis of data, and writing the draft. Baogang Wang, Guiqiang Wen and Yufeng Li: design of investigation, interpretation of data, review and editing the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51975493) and the Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project of China (2015M582567).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lubricating a bright future: Lubrication contribution to energy saving and low carbon emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Cai, R. Guo, F. Zhou, W. Liu, Lubricating a bright future: Lubrication contribution to energy saving and low carbon emission, Sci. China Technol. Sci. 56 (2013) 2888–2913.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tribological performance of silver nanoparticle-enhanced polyethylene glycol lubricants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ghaednia, M.S. Hossain, R.L. Jackson, Tribological Performance of Silver Nanoparticle-Enhanced Polyethylene Glycol Lubricants, Tribol. Trans. 59 (2016) 585–592.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic liquid lubricants: designed chemistry for engineering applications
